tasks.title: Define lease UX status contract and concise blocked responses
tasks.approach: Narrow Phase 93 to the foundation that downstream UX work needs: expose a stable lease status vocabulary and make lease-denied command responses compact, truthful, and actionable. Follow-up tickets in the same phase cover extension controls, bounded wait behavior, and suspect drift detection.
technicalScope: Extend lease status/classification helpers so callers with an agentSessionId can distinguish lease-free, lease-held-by-me, lease-held-by-other, and stale/invalid lease states; reserve suspect/drift semantics for the suspect-drift follow-up ticket.
Revise workspace-edit-lease-held and related non-owner responses to include holder session id, task id when present, expiry, available alternatives, and one recommended next action.
Keep blocked responses compact for agents and avoid suggestions that require broad instruction/catalog reloads or expensive polling.
Document the response/status vocabulary consumed by the extension and follow-up same-phase tickets.
Add focused tests for status classification and denied-response payloads.
acceptanceCriteria: Denied claim, heartbeat, and release paths for active leases held by another session return holder identity, task id when present, expiry, alternatives, and one recommended next action in stable JSON.
A caller-provided agentSessionId can classify lease-free, lease-held-by-me, lease-held-by-other, and stale/invalid states without opening task SQLite or mutating the lease.
Agent-facing blocked copy is concise and does not tell agents to reload broad instructions or poll aggressively.
The shared vocabulary is documented in the relevant command instruction or contract surface for extension consumers.
Automated tests cover same-holder, other-holder, missing, stale/invalid, and compact blocked-response cases.
tasks.summary: Define the shared lease status contract and low-token blocked-response behavior that Phase 93 UX work builds on.
tasks.description: Define the shared lease status contract and low-token blocked-response behavior that Phase 93 UX work builds on.
tasks.risk: Medium: this changes command response contracts that agents and the extension will consume; keep the schema additive and deterministic.

metadata.roadmapSlice: multi-agent workspace lease and task-state authority
goals: Make the safe path feel like guidance instead of a mysterious failure.
Reduce wasted AI credits when an agent cannot edit yet.
Help users recover from stale or suspect lease states without manual file surgery.
blockers: T100188 is completed and provides clone-local lease commands under git common-dir.
Extension work still needs a stable session identifier where available; fallback behavior must be explicit when no session id exists.
The exact edit-capable flows that should honor waitForLease are not enumerated yet; this phase must define the first supported integration points before wiring broad enforcement.
implementationNotes: Use git common-dir for clone-local coordination state; do not store active workspace leases in branch-local task SQLite.
Keep responses compact for agents and avoid broad instruction/catalog reloads in blocked or read-only states.
Prefer fail-safe behavior for file mutation, stash, checkpoint, and task-state authority conflicts.

Current code exposes claim/heartbeat/release/status commands, workspace-coordination-status, a status-bar posture, and a read-only Coordination status card. It does not yet expose held-by-me vs held-by-other status, suspect drift status, extension lease actions, or waitForLease behavior.

ticketReview.verdict: Direction is truthful, but the original ticket was too broad for one implementation task and implied several states/behaviors that are not present yet.
evidence: T100188 is completed with lease command delivery evidence.
src/modules/task-engine/workspace-edit-lease-commands-runtime.ts has claim, heartbeat, release, and workspace-edit-status command behavior.

src/modules/task-engine/coordination/build-workspace-coordination-status.ts emits read-only posture and lease active/stale information, but not holder identity or drift comparison against the lease fingerprint.
extensions/cursor-workflow-cannon/src/extension.ts renders only a compact coordination posture in the status bar.
extensions/cursor-workflow-cannon/src/views/status/render-status-tab.ts renders a read-only Coordination card with lease active/stale/none only.
missingInformation: Which VS Code/session identifier should be treated as the durable agentSessionId for held-by-me vs held-by-other classification.
Which commands or edit-capable flows should accept waitForLease in the first implementation slice.
Whether recover stale and claim/release actions should require explicit per-click policy approval copy in the extension or reuse existing command-client approval handling.
How suspect state should be prioritized relative to dirty_workspace, dirty_task_db, and lease_held posture when multiple signals are true.

tasks.title: Add end-to-end runtime contract regression coverage
tasks.approach: Cover the normal path and failure path across init, launcher, doctor, extension runtime selection, and setup validation.
technicalScope: Test init creates runtime stamp and launcher.
Test launcher uses stamped Node identity.
Test doctor reports healthy and broken runtime contract states.
Test extension prefers stamp or launcher in attached workspaces.
Test setup fails on wrong Node major and passes with native SQLite smoke success.
acceptanceCriteria: Regression tests cover normal attached-workspace command execution through the stamped runtime.
Failure tests cover missing stamp, deleted Node executable, and SQLite load failure.
Extension tests cover stamp-first runtime selection.
The test suite demonstrates that routine Workflow Cannon commands do not rely on shell PATH or editor Node after attach.
tasks.summary: Add integration coverage proving that after attach Workflow Cannon commands use the stamped runtime and no longer depend on arbitrary shell/editor Node selection.

metadata.goal: Lock in the runtime-contract invariant so native SQLite mismatches do not regress.

tasks.title: Add drift and upgrade support for runtime artifacts
tasks.approach: Integrate runtime.json and launcher handling with ownership, drift-check, and upgrade semantics without blindly overwriting environment-specific runtime identity.
technicalScope: Decide which runtime artifacts are kit-owned and which stamp fields are environment-specific.
Add launcher and runtime stamp paths to ownership policy where appropriate.
Update drift-check to detect missing or corrupt launcher and invalid stamp shape.
Update upgrade to regenerate launcher safely.
Avoid overwriting a valid existing runtime stamp with an invalid current shell Node.
acceptanceCriteria: drift-check detects missing or corrupt runtime launcher artifacts.
upgrade can regenerate the launcher without breaking a valid runtime stamp.
Runtime stamp validation distinguishes drift from safe environment-specific identity.
Owned-path metadata accurately reflects managed runtime files.
tasks.summary: Ensure managed runtime artifacts are detected, repaired, and upgraded consistently with other kit-owned files.

metadata.goal: Keep runtime contract artifacts maintainable across upgrades.

tasks.title: Add doctor checks for runtime contract drift
tasks.approach: Use the shared runtime helpers to report whether the installed runtime contract is healthy or has drifted since attach.
technicalScope: Add doctor checks for missing runtime stamp, invalid stamp shape, missing stamped Node executable, wrong Node major, and SQLite load failure.
Check launcher presence and basic integrity.
Report when the current process differs from the stamped runtime.
Include structured runtime contract status in doctor JSON.
Make human output short and repair-oriented.
acceptanceCriteria: workspace-kit doctor reports healthy runtime contract status when stamp and launcher are valid.
doctor catches deleted Node, wrong major, wrong architecture or ABI, missing launcher, and SQLite load failure.
doctor JSON includes machine-readable runtime contract details.
Human remediation names the setup repair path without a long native error matrix.
tasks.summary: Extend doctor to verify the runtime stamp, launcher, stamped Node executable, and native SQLite loadability.

metadata.goal: Detect environment drift after attach without making runtime commands clever.

tasks.title: Update Cursor extension to use runtime stamp first
tasks.approach: Simplify extension command execution so attached workspaces use the deterministic runtime contract, with candidate probing retained only for pre-attach or broken-stamp diagnostics.
technicalScope: Update command client runtime selection to prefer .workspace-kit/runtime.json or .workspace-kit/bin/wk.
Keep WORKSPACE_KIT_NODE and workflowCannon.nodeExecutable as explicit overrides or pre-attach fallback.
Stop using attached project Node markers for normal attached-workspace command execution.
Update extension configuration copy to describe overrides as exceptional.
Add extension tests for stamp-first selection and broken-stamp diagnostics.
acceptanceCriteria: Attached workspaces run extension commands through the stamped Workflow Cannon runtime by default.
The extension does not depend on Electron Node, shell PATH, or attached project Node markers in the normal path.
Broken stamp diagnostics remain structured and actionable.
Existing dashboard/task command tests are updated to cover stamp-first behavior.
tasks.summary: Make the extension prefer the attached workspace runtime stamp or launcher before probing Node candidates.

metadata.goal: Remove editor runtime mismatch as a source of native SQLite failures.

tasks.title: Make workspace-kit init establish runtime contract
tasks.approach: Move runtime validation to the front of init, write the runtime stamp, generate the launcher, and only then initialize planning SQLite.
technicalScope: Update init command flow to validate Node 22 and better-sqlite3 before opening planning stores.
Write .workspace-kit/runtime.json during successful init.
Generate .workspace-kit/bin/wk during successful init.
Show runtime artifacts in init --dry-run --json planned writes.
Make re-running init repair missing launcher or stamp safely.
acceptanceCriteria: workspace-kit init refuses to attach when the runtime contract cannot be validated.
Successful init creates runtime stamp and launcher before SQLite initialization completes.
Re-running init repairs missing runtime artifacts without disturbing user-owned files.
Init JSON output includes runtime contract status and artifact paths.
tasks.summary: Ensure project attach validates and records the Workflow Cannon runtime before SQLite planning initialization.

metadata.goal: Stop bad native SQLite environments at attach time.

tasks.title: Route agent guidance and tasks through canonical launcher
tasks.approach: Change routine attached-project examples to prefer the generated launcher while preserving pnpm exec wk for Workflow Cannon repository development.
technicalScope: Update .ai guidance and command snippets that describe routine attached-project task commands.
Update VS Code task surfaces or generated task guidance where they currently rely on nvm use.
Clarify when pnpm exec wk remains appropriate for Workflow Cannon repo development.
Update native SQLite and install/attach runbook source files.
Avoid editing generated maintainer docs directly unless running the documented docs pipeline.
acceptanceCriteria: Agent-facing attached-project guidance points to .workspace-kit/bin/wk as the canonical command path.
Routine guidance no longer requires the user to run nvm use before Workflow Cannon commands.
Workflow Cannon repo development guidance still permits pnpm exec wk where appropriate.
Docs distinguish attached project runtime from Workflow Cannon runtime.
tasks.summary: Update machine-facing guidance so agents use the launcher after attach instead of environment-sensitive Node or pnpm invocations.

metadata.goal: Prevent agents from bypassing the stamped runtime in normal workflows.

tasks.title: Create canonical Workflow Cannon launcher
tasks.approach: Generate a small launcher that reads the runtime stamp, verifies the stamped Node still exists, and execs the Workflow Cannon CLI with that Node.
technicalScope: Define generated launcher path, likely .workspace-kit/bin/wk for attached workspaces.
Implement launcher generation with portable shell and Windows considerations where appropriate.
Make launcher read runtime.json and exec the stamped nodeExecutable with the package CLI.
Fail clearly if the stamp is missing, stale, or the stamped Node executable is gone.
Add tests proving PATH node and attached project Node markers are ignored.
acceptanceCriteria: .workspace-kit/bin/wk doctor uses the stamped Node executable.
Launcher failure for a missing Node path is short and actionable.
Launcher does not perform broad runtime search after attach.
Launcher does not follow the attached project .nvmrc or .node-version.
tasks.summary: Add a deterministic launcher that runs workspace-kit through the stamped Node executable instead of arbitrary shell or editor Node.

metadata.goal: Make routine agent/editor invocations deterministic after attach.

tasks.title: Enforce Node 22 during package install and dev setup
tasks.approach: Tighten package metadata and setup scripts so a bad runtime cannot produce a misleadingly usable install.
technicalScope: Add package engines for Node 22 and pnpm 10.
Update setup-dev to validate Node major, architecture, ABI, and better-sqlite3 loadability.
Update ensure-native-sqlite to write or refresh the runtime stamp after successful validation.
Keep native rebuild behavior only as install-time repair, not as a recurring runtime strategy.
Update script tests or smoke checks for wrong Node and successful stamped setup.
acceptanceCriteria: setup:dev -- --check-only fails on wrong Node major or native SQLite mismatch.
Successful setup records a runtime stamp.
postinstall validates better-sqlite3 under the active Node 22 runtime.
Failure output points to one setup repair path instead of a broad case matrix.
tasks.summary: Make package install and dev setup fail fast unless the active runtime satisfies the Workflow Cannon Node 22 and native SQLite contract.

metadata.goal: Make the package install environment correct before agents begin work.

tasks.title: Add runtime stamp schema and verification helpers
tasks.approach: Build a small reusable runtime-contract module that centralizes Node identity, stamp parsing, Node 22 validation, and better-sqlite3 smoke checks.
technicalScope: Add a runtime stamp type/schema with nodeExecutable, nodeVersion, arch, platform, abi, packageRoot, and checkedAt.
Reuse or consolidate identity logic from native SQLite diagnostics.
Implement read, write, and verify helpers for the runtime stamp.
Implement a better-sqlite3 smoke check that can run against the intended package root.
Add tests for valid stamp, missing stamp, wrong major, wrong architecture or ABI, and SQLite load failure.
acceptanceCriteria: Runtime stamp helpers are available to scripts, init, doctor, and extension-facing code.
Node 22 validation is enforced by helper tests.
SQLite smoke failure is represented as structured diagnostic data.
No runtime helper mutates task state or opens planning SQLite as part of validation.
tasks.summary: Implement typed helpers for recording and verifying the Workflow Cannon Node runtime identity and native SQLite loadability.

metadata.goal: Give install/init/doctor/launcher one shared source for runtime validation.

tasks.title: Define Workflow Cannon runtime contract
tasks.approach: Write the design contract first so implementation tasks have one source of truth for runtime selection, stamping, launcher behavior, fail-fast setup, and doctor drift reporting.
technicalScope: Document that Workflow Cannon requires Node 22 for CLI/runtime execution.
Define runtime stamp location and fields for attached workspaces.
Define canonical launcher behavior and failure semantics.
Clarify that attached project .nvmrc/.node-version files are not used for Workflow Cannon runtime selection.
Update native SQLite distribution/runbook language to prefer environment correctness over broad runtime recovery.
acceptanceCriteria: A canonical ADR or runbook section describes the Node 22 runtime contract.
The document defines install/init validation, runtime stamping, launcher usage, and doctor drift behavior.
The document explicitly states that Workflow Cannon does not follow attached project Node markers.
Follow-up implementation tasks can cite this document as the runtime contract source.
tasks.summary: Establish the canonical policy that Workflow Cannon owns a Node 22 runtime independent of attached project Node versions.

metadata.goal: Prevent arm64/amd64 better-sqlite3 mismatches by making runtime correctness an install/init invariant.

tasks.title: Detect suspect lease checkout drift
tasks.approach: Compare the active lease fingerprint to the current checkout and surface suspect status when branch, HEAD, worktree, or dirty manifest signals change unexpectedly during a lease.
technicalScope: Compare active lease branch, headSha, worktreePath, and dirtyManifest against the current checkout fingerprint in read-only status paths.
Add stable suspect flags for branch drift, HEAD drift, worktree drift, and dirty manifest drift.
Surface suspect status through workspace-edit-status and workspace-coordination-status without mutating or deleting the lease.
Define posture precedence when suspect signals coexist with dirty workspace, dirty task DB, stale lease, or active lease.
Expose suspect status for extension consumers without requiring raw lease-file parsing.
acceptanceCriteria: Branch, HEAD, worktree path, and dirty manifest changes during an active lease are detected and returned as stable suspect flags.
workspace-edit-status and workspace-coordination-status surface suspect status without mutating the lease file.
Posture precedence is documented and deterministic when multiple coordination signals are present.
The extension-facing payload can render suspect state without reading the lease file directly.
Automated tests cover no drift, branch drift, HEAD drift, dirty manifest drift, stale lease, and malformed lease cases.
tasks.summary: Mark active leases suspect when checkout state drifts from the lease fingerprint.
tasks.description: Mark active leases suspect when checkout state drifts from the lease fingerprint.
tasks.risk: Medium: false positives could make valid work look unsafe; prefer explicit flags over destructive recovery.

metadata.roadmapSlice: multi-agent workspace lease and task-state authority
metadata.blockerReason: Depends on T100189 status vocabulary so suspect can be rendered consistently.
goals: Help users recognize stale or unsafe lease state before mutating files.
Prepare reliable signals for extension rendering and later task-state authority gates.
implementationNotes: Detection must be read-only; recovery remains an explicit command/action.

tasks.title: Add bounded waitForLease behavior
tasks.approach: Define and implement an optional bounded wait path for lease-aware flows so blocked agents can wait cheaply or fall back to read-only planning.
technicalScope: Define waitForLease arguments or helper options, including timeout, interval, and fallback behavior, for the first supported lease-aware entrypoints.
Ensure waiting uses low-frequency checks, bounded timeout, and no tight polling loop.
Return deterministic JSON when the lease becomes available, times out, is stale/recoverable, or remains held by another session.
Keep read-only/plan-only alternatives available when waiting is disabled or times out.
Document which commands or flows support waitForLease in this phase.
acceptanceCriteria: waitForLease is opt-in or conservatively defaulted and has a bounded maximum timeout.
Polling frequency is low and tested, with no busy loop or broad context reload guidance.
Timeout and success responses include the current holder, task id when present, expiry, and recommended next action.
At least one lease-aware command or helper path uses the behavior end to end.
Automated tests cover immediate availability, active-holder timeout, stale/recoverable lease, and disabled/fallback behavior.
tasks.summary: Add a conservative waitForLease path with low-frequency checks and explicit timeout behavior.
tasks.description: Add a conservative waitForLease path with low-frequency checks and explicit timeout behavior.
tasks.risk: Medium: waiting behavior can waste agent credits if defaults are too aggressive.

metadata.roadmapSlice: multi-agent workspace lease and task-state authority
metadata.blockerReason: Depends on T100189 response vocabulary for compact wait and timeout messages.
goals: Reduce wasted AI credits when edits are temporarily blocked.
Give agents a bounded alternative to immediate failure.
implementationNotes: Define supported entrypoints before wiring broad enforcement.

tasks.title: Add extension lease status UI and actions
tasks.approach: Use the T100189 status vocabulary to make lease state visible and actionable in the VS Code extension without requiring users to inspect raw CLI JSON.
technicalScope: Update the status bar, Status tab Coordination card, or dashboard surface to show lease-free, held-by-me, held-by-other, stale/invalid, and suspect-ready display states using compact labels.
Show lease holder session, task id when present, expiry, and stale/invalid state when available from the command contract.
Add extension commands or QuickPick actions for claim, release, recover stale, inspect holder/status, and continue read-only/plan-only.
Pass the best available agentSessionId into lease commands and define a clear fallback when the extension cannot identify the current session.
Keep sensitive lease mutations on the existing policyApproval path and avoid manual lease-file editing guidance.
acceptanceCriteria: The extension UI distinguishes lease-free, lease-held-by-me, lease-held-by-other, stale/invalid, and suspect-ready states without raw JSON inspection.
Users can launch claim, release, recover stale, inspect holder/status, and continue read-only actions from VS Code UI affordances.
Lease actions call workspace-kit commands rather than editing files directly and include policyApproval where the command is sensitive.
When no stable session id is available, the UI explains the limitation through action availability or fallback copy rather than misclassifying ownership.
Extension tests or renderer tests cover the major lease rendering states and at least one action dispatch path.
tasks.summary: Render lease state in the extension and add user actions for common lease operations.
tasks.description: Render lease state in the extension and add user actions for common lease operations.
tasks.risk: Medium: unclear ownership copy could cause users to override leases too casually.

metadata.roadmapSlice: multi-agent workspace lease and task-state authority
metadata.blockerReason: Depends on T100189 status vocabulary and blocked-response contract.
goals: Make lease state understandable inside VS Code.
Provide safe recovery and read-only paths without manual file surgery.
implementationNotes: Prefer existing dashboard/status rendering patterns and command-client paths.

data.listTasksCursorSemantics: Keyset pagination: opaque cursor from prior nextCursor; under concurrent updates rows may move across pages (re-run without cursor for a fresh ordering snapshot).

cae.agentRenderHint: If present, summarize CAE shadow outcome in one short paragraph; when traceId is set, operators can run cae-explain / cae-get-trace.
